title: Chiquita
content:
آ آ آ آ Beautiful! Sir, you may say so. Thar isnï؟½t her match in the county;
آ آ آ آ Is thar, old gal, Chiquita, my darling, my beauty?
آ آ آ آ Feel of that neck, sir, tharï؟½s velvet! Whoa! steady, ah, will you, you vixen!
آ آ آ آ Whoa! I say. Jack, trot her out; let the gentleman look at her paces.

آ آ آ آ Morgan! she ainï؟½t nothing else, and Iï؟½ve got the papers to prove it.
آ آ آ آ Sired by Chippewa Chief, and twelve hundred dollars wonï؟½t buy her.
آ آ آ آ Briggs of Tuolumne owned her. Did you know Briggs of Tuolumne?
آ آ آ آ Busted hisself in White Pine, and blew out his brains down in ï؟½Frisco?

آ آ آ آ Hednï؟½t no savey, hed Briggs. Thar, Jack! thatï؟½ll do, quit that foolinï؟½!
آ آ آ آ Nothinï؟½ to what she kin do, when sheï؟½s got her work cut out before her.
آ آ آ آ Hosses is hosses, you know, and likewise, too, jockeys is jockeys:
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½tainï؟½t evï؟½ry man as can ride as knows what a hoss has got in him.

آ آ آ آ Know the old ford on the Fork, that nearly got Flaniganï؟½s leaders?
آ آ آ آ Nasty in daylight, you bet, and a mighty rough ford in low water!
آ آ آ آ Well, it ainï؟½t six weeks ago that me and the Jedge and his nevey
آ آ آ آ Struck for that ford in the night, in the rain, and the water all round us;

آ آ آ آ Up to our flanks in the gulch, and Rattlesnake Creek just a-bilinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Not a plank left in the dam, and nary a bridge on the river.
آ آ آ آ I had the gray, and the Jedge had his roan, and his nevey, Chiquita;
آ آ آ آ And after us trundled the rocks jest loosed from the top of the canyon.

آ آ آ آ Lickity, lickity, switch, we came to the ford, and Chiquita
آ آ آ آ Buckled right down to her work, and, a fore I could yell to her rider,
آ آ آ آ Took water jest at the ford, and there was the Jedge and me standing,
آ آ آ آ And twelve hundred dollars of hoss-flesh afloat, and a-driftinï؟½ to thunder!

آ آ آ آ Would ye bï؟½lieve it? That night, that hoss, that ï؟½ar filly, Chiquita,
آ آ آ آ Walked herself into her stall, and stood there, all quiet and dripping:
آ آ آ آ Clean as a beaver or rat, with nary a buckle of harness,
آ آ آ آ Just as she swam the Fork, that hoss, that ï؟½ar filly, Chiquita.

آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s what I call a hoss! andآ آ آ آ What did you say?آ آ آ آ Oh, the nevey?
آ آ آ آ Drownded, I reckon, leastways, he never kem beck to deny it.
آ آ آ آ Ye see the derned fool had no seat, ye couldnï؟½t have made him a rider;
آ آ آ آ And then, ye know, boys will be boys, and hosses well, hosses is hosses!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
